---
title: "AI Speech and language variation & change"
subtitle: "A study on manifestation of AI speech in human natural language"
author: "Stephan Schwarz – FU Berlin – 2026"
bibliography: ../drafts/germanic-ai.bib
suppress-bibliography: false
reference-location: document
format:
  html:
    # css: poster_grid.css
    # css: poster.css
    theme: Cosmo
    toc: false
    self-contained: false
    include-before-body: css.html
    # embed-resources: true
  # pdf:
  #   browser: chrome
execute:
  echo: false
---
::: {#logo}
![](fu-logo-250616-RGB-s.svg)
:::

::: {#meta}
class: [16838] germanische sprachen im vergleich  
tutor: Hüning / Konvicna  
term: WS25/26  

-----
![to paper...](qr_ogy-de-study-ai-speech.png)
authored: st. schwarz  
snc: #16115::`r Sys.time()`   
[<<< back to paper <<<](https://esteeschwarz.github.io/DC-LX/essais/germanic/001/gemini.html)
:::

```{r loads, echo=FALSE, warning=F,output=F}
#script_lines <- readLines("../LLM-003.R")
load("../drafts/plist2.RData")
```

::: {#code-container}

```{r background-script, echo=FALSE, results='asis'}
script_lines <- readLines("../LLM-003.R")
# load("../drafts/plist2.RData")

cat("```r\n")
s<-sample(1:length(script_lines),80)
# s<-1:100
# cat(paste(s," - ",script_lines[s], collapse = "\n"))
cat(paste(script_lines[s], collapse = "\n"))
cat("\n```")
```

<!-- footer: snc.16093.1/107*162 -->
:::
```{r prompt, echo=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# prompt <- readLines("../g-prompt01.txt")
#prompt<-paste(prompt,collapse = "\n")
prompt<-plist$ptx
# prompt<-paste0("C1: ",paste(prompt,collapse = "\n"))
prompt<-paste0("",paste(prompt,collapse = "\n"))

```

::: {.column-1}
# Preliminary
Inspired by @bsi_wie_2025 and @yakura_empirical_2025 which found evidence for GPT influenced human language after the introduction of chatGPT we tried to replicate the @yakura_empirical_2025 pipeline of building an AI vocabulary (model preferred lemmata) and compare frequencies of model typical words across pre- and post model introduction human language corpora. The first draft essai proves their hypothesis that LLM generated language manifests within human natural language.

# Questions / Hypotheses

- do humans adapt to language produced by LLM and incorporate model vocabulary into their own language production?
- H1: we will find higher frequencies of LLM typical vocabulary within human natural language corpora after onset of model introduction.

# Methods / Data

Our human languaga data consists of raw texts from german bundestag plenary protocols, @dip_dip_2026. 

The LLM corpus consists of  model summaries of a first subset of these texts generated with the prompt (to gemini API, @wikipedia_google_2026) you see in C1.

We first devised model typical lemmata in the AI corpus which are distinct for that corpus using their log odds score calculated over pre/post target, cf. @fig-gpt.dplot.

For corpus building and evaluation scripts cf. @schwarz_this_2026.

# c1 {.bw}
`r prompt`
:::

::: {.column-2}

# p2
```{r}
#| fig-cap: "stats descriptive"
#| label: "fig-boxplot01"
#| echo: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE


#knitr::include_graphics("../../../q/germanic/001/gemini_files/figure-html/fig-boxplot01-1.png")
plist$boxdesc2 #TODO
```

# p3
```{r,eval=T}
#| fig-cap: "lemma frequency increase"
#| label: "fig-barplot"
#| echo: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE

# knitr::include_graphics("../../../q/germanic/001/gemini_files/figure-html/fig-densplot01-1.png")

plist$barplot
b1<-plist$b1
lb0<-plist$lb0
lb1<-length(b1)
```

# c2: corpus stats {.ct}
<!-- ``` `r knitr::kable(plist$boxstat)` ``` -->
```{r,eval=T}
#| label: "boxstats"
#| fig-cap: "statistics..."
#| warning: false
#| echo: false

# knitr::kable(plist$sdf)
knitr::kable(plist$boxstat)
```

```{r,eval=F}
#| fig-cap: "model vocab density"
#| label: "fig-densplot01"
#| echo: FALSE

# knitr::include_graphics("../../../q/germanic/001/gemini_files/figure-html/fig-densplot01-1.png")

plist$gplot
```

# c3: regression simple model {.bws}

```{r,eval=T,warning=F} 
#knitr::kable(plist$sdf)
print(summary(plist$lme$lm2))
anova(plist$lme$lm2)

```


# p4
```{r}
#| fig-cap: "model vocab distribution"
#| label: "fig-gpt.dplot"
#| echo: FALSE
#| warning: FALSE

#knitr::include_graphics("../../../q/germanic/001/gemini_files/figure-html/fig-gpt.dplot-1.png")
 plist$gptplot2 #todo
```
:::

::: {.column-3}
# Results
Yet with simple descriptive stats comparing the raw frequencies of gpt-preferred lemmas in pre- and post-gemini onset we find that in the target corpus the occurences of these lemma increase, see @fig-boxplot01.

The `r lb1` lemma where 1. the relative frequency of lemma in target=post is higher than in target=pre (n=`r lb0`) and 2. that frequency exceeds the mean relative frequency is displayed in @fig-barplot.

We observe significant frequency increases using a linear regression model (@bates_fitting_2015), cf. C3/C4, which tested the hypothesis.


# c4: regression random effects model {.bws}

```{r,eval=T,warning=F,message=F} 
#knitr::kable(plist$sdf)
print(summary(plist$lme$lm1))
anova(plist$lme$lm1)
s1<-summary(plist$lme$lm1)
s2<-summary(plist$lme$lm2)
c1<-s1$coefficients
c2<-s2$coefficients
c2<-round(c2,2)
c1<-round(c1,2)
#c2
#s2
```

# model explanations {.expl}
We see in the model summaries that frequency increases are significant (p<0.001) when positing a random effect of lemma (model 1, cf. C4) with an estimate increase of mean frequency (pmw) per lemma by `r c1[3,1]` vs. the intercept (post=FALSE i.e. target[pre]). 

In model 2, cf. C3 (simple model without lemma effect) we observe an estimate increase of mean frequency (pmw) of post=TRUE by `r c2[2,1]` vs. the intercept.

In both models `gp` (independent var of above mentioned gpt score: higher score > more model typical lemma) has been included as fixed effect, but vcvs. without random lemma effect that predictor lets the model naturally collapse (p<10E-16).


# Limitations
If the findings of increased AI lemma frequencies really prove manifestation of AI language depends also on influences of other factors of language variation and change which @yakura_empirical_2025 already took into account. A deeper going analysis of general trends in language development and investigation on the bidirectional influences of model-training and human language could harden the results. 

:::{#refs}
# references
:::
:::
